evaluation_meta.notes: Reference worked example review for the first higher-mode fairness/process-breakdown divorce slice.

C4.note: The run does not merely summarize the conflict; it attempts repair, slows the exchange, and treats interruption as a real process variable.

C5.note: Option generation remains intentionally limited because the system correctly chooses co-handling before further autonomous option work.

C6.note: The run treats participation fairness as architecture-relevant and does not confuse domination with mere clarity.

C9.note: Escalation is selected at the point where repair has failed rather than too early or too late.

P5.note: The shared divorce layer and slice policy preserve a fairness/process-breakdown story rather than collapsing the case into a generic disagreement.

I3.note: Trace, flags, summary, and escalation all preserve the same co-handling story.

I4.note: Qualification, process-breakdown detection, and the final M3 action remain well aligned.

I6.note: The slice is easy to review as a higher-mode fairness/process-breakdown case without transcript dependence.

ES5_handoff_review_quality.note: The run clearly preserves why co-handling became necessary even though the active reference profile does not persist continuity artifacts.

escalation_review.notes: The system correctly moves from repair attempt to M3 co-handling once the participation imbalance remains active and a human path is explicitly endorsed, while preserving continuity-aware process-breakdown reasoning across the evaluator-visible artifact set.

final_judgment.short_rationale: The run preserves the process-breakdown story clearly, attempts repair before escalating, and reaches a defensible M3 co-handling posture once participation fairness is not restored.
final_judgment.follow_up_recommendation: Use this reference evaluation as the first higher-mode benchmark anchor and compare future escalation-sensitive slices against it for threshold and handoff quality.
